content:
Have you ever taken a walk in the English countryside? Have you seen the gentle wrinkles in the soil, felt them rise and fall almost imperceptibly under your feet as you wonder what the sheep think of you walking right through their breakfast, lunch, and dinner? Have you wondered, “Why does the soil do that?” Even if you have never been to England, you may know the answer; and even if you do not know the answer, and you come from some far-away land, this wrinkled soil is a part of your own history, because it is a part of the history of capitalism, and thus the quick and quickening loss of whatever is left of the commons.
“The commons.” A term you may have first heard following either “the house of” or “a tragedy of” or even, these days, with “atmospheric” wedged into the middle of the phrase. But cast your mind back to a time before the English soil wrinkled, before the wealthy stole the original commons. Cast your mind back to a rural field somewhere in Gloucestershire - and if you don’t know quite where that is, then picture the Shire. Whether you now have a medieval peasant in your mind, or a Hobbit, the scene is much the same: Common folk working the land. In Spring, they hitch the plows to the horses and drag them across the fields; they walk the fields scattering seeds by hand. In summer, haymaking - hear it in your mind, the soft rustle and an almost imperceptible metallic ringing as the mowers walk rhytmically through the grass, their scythes skimming the soil and laying down the crop in neat bundles. Come autumn, they harvest what is left; they walk the orchards gathering windfall apples for cider, they prepare for winter. And in winter - well, there was not much left to do, so it was a time to repair tools damaged throughout the year, but also to hunker down, appreciate community, and celebrate as the days once again start to get longer and brighter.
Did you catch the word, by the way? “Common” folk? Another term, of course, is “commoners,” and you will find this word still in use in its original sense in some places, such as the New Forest in the south of England, where the famous “wild” ponies are all owned by the New Forest commoners: The people who live in the New Forest, and who have the right to turn their horses loose on common ground. And this is exactly what the word meant to the rural people of medeival England, too: They worked common fields, and, if they were lucky enough to be able to afford livestock, turned them out into common fields. The King, not the local lords, owned the land; and while the lords had the right to charge rent, they had no right to evict peasants from the common fields without strong legal justification.
Oh - and those wrinkles in the soil? Those are the remants of the old “ridge and furrow” plowing, a result of decades and centuries over which families plowed the same strips in the same fields.
We should pause here and acknowledge that life was not all roses for the medieval peasant. They had a right to the land, yes; but they also had a legal obligation to workd the local lord’s fields, and were barred from moving away without the lord’s consent. And, of course, “the tragedy of the commons” originates as a critique of this system, with some economists arguing that by being commonly held, each commoner sought to maximize their own outcome, leading to widespread overgrazing - the claim being that “unmanaged” resources tend to be overused resources. The reality was rather different, and Garrett Hardin, the originator of the phrase, was quite explicit that the fields were not unmanaged, but communally managed, which could have either good or bad outcomes, depending on the community. Still, it would be wrong to pretend there were no bad outcomes.
By the late medieval period, though, the lords realized that they could extract more value with more rigid control. New approaches could produce more crops in less time; and if they took the fields where peasants grew their own food, they could not only turn out the sheep there - producing valuable wool - but turn the agricultural peasants into laborers. Never mind that this led to the deaths of thousands, perhaps hundreds of thousands. Consider that the population of England in the wake of the Black Death was still perhaps 2.5 million. This population continued to decline by another 20% to 2 million by the mid-1400s, and ultimately grew very little until the late 1500s. Hundreds of villages wholly disappeared during this period, unable to sustain themselves.
The Tudor Kings saw the problem - as a threat to their own wealth, of course, as declining population meant declining revenues. And a moral panic began to grow about homelessness. Tillage acts were passed by the crown, limiting and sometimes even outright banning enclosures. But quis custodiet ipsos custodes? Local law enforcement was the responsibility of those very same people who were enclosing the land and evicting the peasants.
Ultimately, this tipping of the scales - away from the commoners (a name only by now) and toward the nobility, now becoming every wealthier. That wealth, in ever greater excees, ultimately formed the monetary basis of capitalism. Capitalism did not replace the agrarian society; no, hatred of the poor did that job just fine. Capitalism was what came next.
title: Modern Enclosure
